source_paper_title: Extension of Additive Valuations to General Valuations on the Existence of EFX

context:
Page 2, Introduction (discussion of prior work and open status of EFX existence for general valuations)

Despite significant effort, we were not able to settle the question of whether an EFX allocation always exists (assuming all goods must be allocated), and leave it as an enigmatic open question.
It is not known whether EFX allocations always exist even when \(n = 3\) for general valuations.
title: Prove existence of complete EFX allocations for general valuations setting
background_latex:
Let there be a finite set of indivisible items \(M\) and a finite set of agents \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\). Each agent \(i\in N\) has a valuation function \(v_i:2^M\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) satisfying:
1) Normalization: \(v_i(\varnothing)=0\).
2) Monotonicity: if \(S\subseteq T\subseteq M\), then \(v_i(S)\le v_i(T)\).
An allocation is an \(n\)-tuple \(X=(X_1,\dots,X_n)\) of pairwise-disjoint bundles \(X_i\subseteq M\). It is complete if \(\bigcup_{i\in N} X_i=M\).
For agents \(i,j\in N\), agent \(i\) \emph{envies} agent \(j\) under allocation \(X\) if \(v_i(X_i) < v_i(X_j)\).
An allocation \(X\) is \emph{envy-free up to any item (EFX)} if for every pair \(i,j\in N\) and every item \(g\in X_j\),
\[
 v_i(X_i) \ge v_i(X_j\setminus\{g\}).
\]
Equivalently, after removing any single item from \(j\)'s bundle, agent \(i\) does not prefer the remainder to her own bundle.
The central general-valuation setting imposes no further structure such as additivity, submodularity, or identical valuations beyond normalization and monotonicity.
problem_definition_latex:
Given any numbers of agents \(n\ge 1\), any finite item set \(M\), and any collection of normalized, monotone valuation functions \(\{v_i:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\}_{i\in N}\), determine whether there must exist a complete allocation \(X=(X_1,\dots,X_n)\) of \(M\) that is EFX, i.e., satisfies
\[
\forall i,j\in N,\ \forall g\in X_j:\quad v_i(X_i) \ge v_i(X_j\setminus\{g\}).
\]
In particular, resolve the existence question for general valuations when all goods must be allocated.
latex:
Let there be a finite set of indivisible items \(M\) and a finite set of agents \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\). Each agent \(i\in N\) has a valuation function \(v_i:2^M\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) satisfying:
1) Normalization: \(v_i(\varnothing)=0\).
2) Monotonicity: if \(S\subseteq T\subseteq M\), then \(v_i(S)\le v_i(T)\).
An allocation is an \(n\)-tuple \(X=(X_1,\dots,X_n)\) of pairwise-disjoint bundles \(X_i\subseteq M\). It is complete if \(\bigcup_{i\in N} X_i=M\).
For agents \(i,j\in N\), agent \(i\) \emph{envies} agent \(j\) under allocation \(X\) if \(v_i(X_i) < v_i(X_j)\).
An allocation \(X\) is \emph{envy-free up to any item (EFX)} if for every pair \(i,j\in N\) and every item \(g\in X_j\),

The central general-valuation setting imposes no further structure such as additivity, submodularity, or identical valuations beyond normalization and monotonicity.

Given any numbers of agents \(n\ge 1\), any finite item set \(M\), and any collection of normalized, monotone valuation functions \(\{v_i:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\}_{i\in N}\), determine whether there must exist a complete allocation \(X=(X_1,\dots,X_n)\) of \(M\) that is EFX, i.e., satisfies
\[
\forall i,j\in N,\ \forall g\in X_j:\quad v_i(X_i) \ge v_i(X_j\setminus\{g\}).
\]
In particular, resolve the existence question for general valuations when all goods must be allocated.
location_in_paper: Page 2, Introduction (discussion of prior work and open status of EFX existence for general valuations)
open_problem_quote:
Despite significant effort, we were not able to settle the question of whether an EFX allocation always exists (assuming all goods must be allocated), and leave it as an enigmatic open question.
It is not known whether EFX allocations always exist even when \(n = 3\) for general valuations.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: A counterexample search is conceivable by enumerating small \((n,m)\) and restricting valuations to a finite parametric family (e.g., integer-valued monotone functions) and then solving a feasibility check for EFX allocations, but the space of monotone set functions is enormous and requires strong discretization assumptions to be computationally tractable.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can encode candidate hard instances (finite valuation tables with monotonicity constraints) and use a fitness signal based on solving an exact/ILP or SAT check for existence of an EFX allocation, enabling an evolutionary search for potential counterexamples or tight families. However, without additional structure, the search space is very large and success may depend on clever instance parameterizations and efficient feasibility solvers.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The complete-allocations EFX existence question for general (only normalized and monotone) valuations remains open in full generality, and is not known even for \(n=3\). The foundational work of Plaut and Roughgarden establishes the main tractable special cases in this general model—complete EFX for \(n=2\) and for identical valuations—and also highlights algorithmic barriers via strong value-query lower bounds. Subsequent progress has largely proceeded by importing structural tools from the additive setting, especially the champion graph and Pareto-improvable (PI) cycles, which allow one to transform a partial EFX allocation into another EFX allocation that improves a carefully chosen potential.

Mahara (2021) is the main advance within the general-valuation model: it extends additive-technique results to show complete EFX existence in two notable regimes—when agents’ valuations come from only two valuation functions, and when the number of items is small (\(m\le n+3\)). It also strengthens the best-known “EFX with charity” guarantee for general valuations from \(|U|\le n-1\) (Chaudhury et al. 2020) to \(|U|\le n-2\), while ensuring no agent envies the unallocated set \(U\). Technically, this relies on champion-graph reasoning plus two potential functions: the lexicographic potential (as in the \(n=3\) additive existence proof of Chaudhury, Garg, and Mehlhorn) and a new partition-leximin potential tailored to the two-valuations regime.

Despite these advances, the gap to full generality is substantial: Mahara also constructs a \(n=3\) general-valuation instance showing that the lexicographic-potential progress approach can get stuck, indicating that new ideas are needed beyond current champion-graph/potential-function frameworks. Promising directions include identifying additional valuation structure short of additivity that still supports monotone potential progress, strengthening PI-cycle existence arguments in generalized champion graphs, or developing existence proofs that avoid algorithmic progress measures altogether (e.g., via topological or combinatorial fixed-point style arguments adapted to discrete allocations).

sources.title: Almost envy-freeness with general valuations
sources.theorem_or_result: For general (normalized, monotone) valuations, proves existence of complete EFX allocations for (i) two agents and (ii) identical valuations; also shows strong query-complexity lower bounds for finding EFX even in restricted cases, and proves existence of partial EFX allocations leaving up to \(n-1\) items unallocated with a non-envy-to-charity guarantee.
sources.connection: This is the foundational positive evidence and barrier result for the open question of complete EFX existence under general valuations; it establishes the main known solvable special cases and motivates the need for new techniques beyond value-query algorithms.

sources.title: EFX exists for three agents
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves existence of complete EFX allocations for \(n=3\) under additive valuations via the champion graph and a lexicographic potential argument that maintains EFX while making progress.
sources.connection: Although limited to additive valuations, it introduces the champion-graph/PI-cycle framework and lexicographic potential method that Mahara adapts to general valuations for partial-allocation results and small-\(m\) regimes.

sources.title: A little charity guarantees almost envy-freeness
sources.theorem_or_result: For general valuations, establishes existence of partial EFX allocations with a set \(U\) of unallocated items such that no agent envies \(U\), with \(|U|\le n-1\).
sources.connection: Directly addresses the general-valuation setting by proving an 'EFX with charity' guarantee; Mahara strengthens the bound to \(|U|\le n-2\), but the complete-allocation existence remains open.

sources.title: Extension of Additive Valuations to General Valuations on the Existence of EFX
sources.theorem_or_result: For general valuations, proves: (i) complete EFX exists when agents have one of two valuations; (ii) complete EFX exists when \(m\le n+3\); (iii) EFX with charity exists with \(|U|\le n-2\) and no agent envies \(U\); and gives an \(n=3\) example showing limitations of lexicographic-potential progress.
sources.connection: This is the source paper framing the open problem; it provides the strongest known general-valuation existence results short of full generality and develops the partition-leximin potential technique.

sources.title: Existence of EFX for two additive valuations
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows that for additive valuations, complete EFX allocations exist when all agents' valuations come from a set of two additive valuation functions.
sources.connection: Precursor to Mahara (2021): motivates the 'two valuations' tractable regime and informs the general-valuation extension via new potential functions.

sources.title: (almost full) EFX exists for four agents (and beyond)
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops generalized champion-graph machinery and proves near-complete EFX guarantees (e.g., for \(n=4\) with at most one unallocated item) and broader valuation classes (nice cancelable valuations) with \(|U|\le n-2\) unallocated items.
sources.connection: Provides key technical tools (Pareto-improvable cycles and generalized champion graphs) used by Mahara to extend 'charity' and other results to general valuations; still does not resolve complete EFX existence for arbitrary general valuations.

sources.title: Envy-freeness up to any item with high Nash welfare: The virtue of donating items
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces and studies 'EFX with charity' (donating/unallocated items) and shows existence of partial EFX allocations with strong welfare guarantees under additive valuations.
sources.connection: Establishes the charity relaxation framework that later work (including Chaudhury et al. 2020 and Mahara 2021) extends to general valuations and tighter bounds on the number of unallocated items.
